title: How a Moscow Patriarchate spelling decision propagates to half a billion non-English speakers
subtitle: An editorial choice on mospat.ru flows through five amplification layers into Spanish, Portuguese, and African media networks invisible to English-language corpora — and from there into LLM training data.

source.label: Moscow Patriarchate (mospat.ru)
source.sublabel:
Department for External Church Relations
6,000+ English articles since 2015

tiers.title: Tier 1 · Direct ROC structures
tiers.subtitle: Russian Orthodox Church bodies that take editorial cues from Moscow

nodes.label: Patriarchal Exarchate of Africa
nodes.note: Created in 2021 in direct rivalry with the Constantinople-aligned Patriarchate of Alexandria; one of the fastest-growing ROC structures in history

nodes.label: ROCOR Diocese of Caracas & South America
nodes.note: Headquartered at the Holy Trinity Cathedral in Buenos Aires; covers all of South America

nodes.label: iglesiarusa.info
nodes.note: Where Moscow Patriarchate decisions reach the Spanish web first

tiers.title: Tier 2 · Russian state media in Spanish/Portuguese
tiers.subtitle: Ideologically aligned amplifiers with massive social reach

nodes.label: RT en Español
nodes.note: 3rd most-shared domain on Twitter for Spanish posts about the Ukraine war (after YouTube and El País)

nodes.label: Sputnik Mundo
nodes.note: Top-15 most-shared on Spanish Twitter for war coverage

nodes.label: LatamNews.lat
nodes.note: Launched in 2024 to circumvent Western sanctions on Sputnik. Sputnik mirror with new domain

nodes.label: Russkiy Mir Foundation
nodes.note: Putin-decreed soft-power agency. Centers in Argentina (×2), Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, Costa Rica, Guatemala, Nicaragua, Cuba, Mexico

tiers.title: Tier 3 · Spanish-language Catholic media that cover ROC news
tiers.subtitle: Influential Spanish/Portuguese Catholic outlets that publish stories about Patriarch Kirill, the ROC, and the Ukraine war

nodes.label: ACI Prensa
nodes.note: Largest Spanish-language Catholic news agency (EWTN-affiliated). Dedicated 'Iglesia Ortodoxa Rusa' topic page

nodes.label: Religión Digital
nodes.metric: Dedicated 'Papa e Iglesia ortodoxa rusa' topic
nodes.note: Major Spanish Catholic news site. Multiple Kirill exposés (KGB ties, war support, arrest warrant)

nodes.label: CNN en Español
nodes.metric: Latin America's largest English-origin news network
nodes.note: Reproduces religion-and-war coverage in Spanish across LATAM

tiers.title: Tier 4 · Audience exposed (none in our 8 corpus sources)
tiers.subtitle: Populations downstream of the propagation chain who get their information about Ukraine in Spanish, Portuguese, or via Orthodox channels

nodes.label: Latin American Catholics
nodes.note: The largest Catholic population on earth. Catholic news in Spanish/Portuguese is their primary source for religion-related current affairs

nodes.label: African Christians in ROC-active countries
nodes.note: Estimate: ROC presence is concentrated in countries with large Christian populations (Nigeria, DRC, Uganda, Kenya, etc.)

nodes.label: Spanish + Portuguese Wikipedia
nodes.metric: Both languages cite Spanish Catholic media as sources
nodes.note: Wikipedia citations propagate non-English framings into LLM training data via Common Crawl

nodes.label: LLM training corpora (Common Crawl, FineWeb, RefinedWeb)
nodes.metric: Multilingual scrape of the open web
nodes.note: Spanish/Portuguese Catholic news sites are crawled and end up in LLM training data, contaminating spelling preferences with pre-2022 forms

key_finding: Even if we found no Catholic Latin American outlet that copy-pastes mospat.ru directly, the structural pipeline is in place: Russia's Spanish-language state media (RT en Español alone has 18 M followers — almost twice Constantinople's entire English readership) coordinates editorially with the Moscow Patriarchate. Catholic outlets like ACI Prensa and Religión Digital report on ROC positions because Kirill is a major religious newsmaker. The reach asymmetry is the story: a single editorial choice on mospat.ru can be amplified to ~500 million Spanish/Portuguese speakers — roughly the entire English-speaking world — while our 8 corpus sources see none of it.
